title: 2025/11/13 - TI
description: Thursday 13 November 2025 - Transport and Infrastructure Committee Local Government (Auckland Council) (Transport Governance) Amendment Bill Hearing of Evidence: Auckland Council Mayor Wayne Brown Andy Baker Phil Wilson Megan Tyler Bus and Coach Association Delaney Myers Max Marshall Connor Sharp PSA Te Pukenga Here Tikanga Sonja Lister Benedict Ferguson Tom Buckley Mahurangi Community Planning Group Bill Foster New Zealand Automobile Association Martin Glynn The Campaign for Better Transport Incorporated Jodi Johnston Auckland One Rail Lisa D'Oliveira Aalap Patel Brian Ferguson Graeme Easte
descriptionSummary: Transport and Infrastructure Committee. Local Government (Auckland Council) (Transport Governance) Amendment Bill. Auckland Council

chunks.text: Dialogue and questions, so in your hands. Well, that's good. Really. I thought you might come up to Auckland just once in a blue moon, but, Oh, you know, look, you know, I've been there more than you've been to Wellington, Wayne. Well, it's possibly true, but I'm not submitting on anything in Wellington. You are here, but nevertheless, the digital version is quite good for me.

Anyhow, um, I've got the Chair of Council's transport committee, who's this gentleman here and beside me, Andrew Baker, and a couple of Congratulations. Thank Your honor. Uh, and I'd like to congratulate the government for getting this bill into the house And the opposition parties for supporting it through its first read.

So please get on with it. And we know how to find that. We have to wait for several years, like the, um, time of use charging. This is urgent. It'll all help you at the next election from whichever side you are from. Auckland is overwhelmingly want to see a change in the way that the transport operates in Auckland.

It's overwhelming. The biggest issue raised with me by the public when I'm out and about. And probably the same for your Auckland mps as well. My plan to fix Auckland Transport carried me to a large victory. Some of the issues come through a longstanding underinvestment and infrastructure and lack of proper public transport over this, but we're getting on top of that with big projects like City Rail Link.

And I thank all parties for their support and getting that project close to the finish line. However, Auckland has remained fed up with the small issues and the decisions that are made by Auckland Transport on a day-to-day basis for things that are around them. Auckland Transport operate, operates very differently to any other council transport department in New Zealand as a statutory independent that operates an arms length from the council.

I've tried every lever at my disposal to get 'em to follow council direction, but haven't made much progress. The only real power I have is to send them stern letters, which are mostly ignored or never get down to the people doing the work or the consultants that they use instead of doing it themselves. The independence leads to Democratic deficit and transport decision making that is at the heart of many issues we see in transport here today.

Every day, even the streets right outside my office, Victoria and Willis Street, we've examples of how the current system is broken. There's no joined up thinking and no political oversight, and it results in stupid things that annoy everyone. I've taken our senior people and their senior people just the two intersections around here, and we've pointed out that every single thing that they have done is wrong.

And my, our leaders up here in the council suddenly woken up that fact as well. The bill fixes this issue of independence by focusing it on the delivery of public transport service services, which is not bad at leaving council how transport should function in a, it's nothing radical.

It's exactly how transport operates in every other council in New Zealand, the public understand things pretty well. Public trans, um, satisfaction rates are high for public transport side of it up in the eighties and nineties, but they're down in the twenties for everything else that at does.

So the structure and the bill lines up well with the problems that we see and the public. The bill also sets up a new structure for collaboration between council and central government. And for the first time, we'll have an integrated 30 year transport plan for the region. But that does mean integrating. That means that the government will have to understand that we will be part of that.

I've been very frustrated by the approach of success of governments to transport in Auckland. Both government have announced projects costing tens of billion dollars without taking talking to us. The labor ended into trouble because the projects were poorly thought out, development secret and cost more. And so we never even started them, and the parties ended up embarrassing themselves.

But same with this lot. There's no, we want no more secret harbor crossings, or I had my very first briefing about the harbor crossing yesterday, my very first briefing, which was deeply embarrassing to the people who presented to me because it wasn't well thought through.

Meanwhile, Auckland have been left disillusioned by promises of long-term transport projects that never eventuate. The small projects that actually fix problems are ignored. The approaches must change. And this is why I've been pushing for an integrated 30 year plan and a joint council government committee. It'll be good New plan and regional transport committee should minimize political squabbling and set up the long term processes.

The 30 year plan will be based on logical assessment of the movement of goods and people, and not on the reelection of mps. NZTA will have to get used to being us being involved. I should caution though that the legislation around transport and local government is very complicated.

Our legal and government sub teams have been through in a high level of detail. They found some areas where provisions of the bill have hardwired in legislation that might make things too bureaucratic. Willington has a tendency to over be, be overly prescriptive in legislation. This may look great to you, but it often leads to provisions that are unworkable or add paperwork for the sake of it.

I don't wanna be back in a year's time asking for urgent legislative fix because we've found transport projects are struggling to get built because some clever lawyer finds a criminal and wants the ACT's enforce. One area we're concerned about is the delegation of powers to local boards. Keep in mind, we have 21 local boards.

Most of them are populations bigger than parts of the HU Valley. I want communities to be able to make decisions over local streets. They're the ones using them every day, but we don't need to spell out pri and primary law exactly what powers full council has and what powers local boards have.

We're the experts in Auckland, so allow us the ability to delegate powers appropriately, just like Christchurch does with its community. Boards will be accountable for that. The bill also says that every single local board has to agree to a bylaw, and that's just ridiculous. That power should sit with a governing body and the board should be consulted and not have veto powers under that rule.

The local board at great barrier could have a veto power on things in the city. We wouldn't give each dis, you wouldn't give each district or city council the power to veto laws that you pass, for example. I also think there is a bit of government overreach in a few places.

The bill requires ministerial approval for several matters, such as delegation to the transport CCO, and the Road classification network. Now, I know ministers love giving themselves extra power, but we should ask us if this is really necessary. Ministers have better things to do than to prove if the bullet track should be controlled by the governing body or by the local board.

On our pass on to our staff team leading the transport reform, including some, um, it says excellent lawyers, but I think they're quite good. Might be a better description from council. I'm not normally mad about lawyers or bureaucrats, but this team are knowledgeable, so I recommend that you listen to them. And I'll also pass over to my chief executive, Phil Winston, who will probably Phil Wilson, who will repeat what I've said, but in sort of bureaucrat.

So I hope you're in fluent in that. But that's the blood version basically. It's good. Basically, we like separating out and leaving behind public transport, which they're not bad at. And basically, we really wanna get on with the risk of things and make as many decisions as we can ourselves and trust the fact some of us know quite a lot about things.

I know a lot about roading. I know nothing about choreography, but I know a lot abouting. Trust us. Right? Thank you. Thank you, Wayne. You've choreographed that very well. All the same. Right? Let's do some other intros as well there. So, uh, and, um, so we we'll hear from the officials.

Cool. Please step forward into the law, into the central position. Um, and, uh, good morning everyone. Um, I'll try and limit the, uh, bureaucrat. Was that the word? Uh, mayor Wayne? Um, I don't think I'm, Um, and, um, just to, just to note, I'm joined, um, on my left, your right.

Um, uh, Megan Tyler, who's, uh, um, our executive team. Morning. Um, look, I won't repeat the mayor. Um, um, what the mayor said too much, um, just to acknowledge I'm the I am Council's chief executive and, and therefore have a pretty vital interest in this reform in the sense that it creates a new CCO that we've got a key relationship with.

Um, but importantly, um, a quite significant chunk of transport activity envisaged. If we, if we just presume for a moment that the default position in the legislation holds, then, uh, you've got quite a significant function coming into, uh, into my organization.

So, um, um, just starting at a high level, as the mere says, um, we broadly welcome the change and the opportunity for a reset. Um, um, and, and for the reasons that are articulated in the, in the forward to the legislation in which we ourselves have, um, advocated for, for a period of time in terms of strength and democratic accountability, a level of local determination around certain, uh, um, transport matters with a bit of, and Megan will talk to this with a bit of caution because of course, the transport system needs to operate,

um, effectively regionally as a network to a significant degree as well. So we need this, we need this complementarity between the local decision making and boards and what exists, um, regionally, we don't think that's totally right in the way that it's framed in the legislation.

Um, but a bit of, bit of a few tweaks will, will help, um, at a more strategic level. Um, you know, I I want to endorse two things really. Um, the importance of integrating transport planning, you know, with our other land use and growth and capital infrastructure, um, planning, um, that's, that's vital in order that, um, the city develops in a, in a coherent and integrated manner because we cannot think about, um, transport investments separate from land use and separate from other infrastructure investments, water

and wastewater in particular. So it's a statement perhaps of the bleeding obvious. Um, but the advent of the, um, control over the destiny of transport in the region for that reason alone is, is quite vital. Um, I think that the second thing is to acknowledge the, um, at a governance level, the notion of, um, council and the crown being parties to the regional transport committee and that high level decision making for the region, um, is, is welcome.

You know, we are both, broadly speaking, equal funding partners in, in this journey, and it, it holds therefore that, um, joint input to some of that high level decision making is good for the future. There are some who have criticized council for surrendering, a little bit of sovereignty in that regard.

Um, but, um, we don't particularly see it that way. We see it as an opportunity actually for council and the Crown to be joined up, um, for there to be a more durable transport plan for Auckland that, um, if you like, better survives political cycles, um, and, and clarifies and really aligns priorities going forward that, um, to my mind is, um, closer to Nirvana and the transport space, um, given the, its critical importance to the way that Auckland, uh, develops and functions as a city.

Um, so, so we welcome those measures. There are a number of things that we are suggesting, um, through the submission, and I'm just gonna ask Megan to, um, speak to some of those more specific items. And then I'm gonna come back, um, myself and just talk a little bit to some of the transition provisions.

Um, you know, um, it doesn't escape me that, um, um, I, we have a particular responsibility from an employment, uh, perspective here. Um, and we've gotta have a really, um, positive experience of transitioning to these new, uh, arrangements.

And I've personally got a legal responsibility in that regard. So I'd like to return to that, but only after me, Megan has spoken to a couple of the specifics. Uh, thanks. Uh, thank you Chair, foster. Thank you, Phil. Thank you. And, uh, Morina. Coto. Good morning. Thank you.

Um, Mr. Chair, I'll just, uh, go through a few of the details briefly. The mayor has, has kind of referenced some of them, uh, but just to be clear, the reason that we are raising this is to make this workable. So it's important for both, uh, you as government, uh, and us as Auckland Council, that these provisions are workable.

And that's, uh, the reason and, and where we've come from, um, in making our submission. Firstly, in terms of the transport, CCO, um, obviously the bill is, uh, drafted that, uh, it is a, a statutory CCO that will have public transport, um, uh, functionality.

Our position here is that we've consistently said the government doesn't have to force Auckland Council to, um, to go into a CCO. Uh, we can make that decision ourselves. Uh, and so we should really be enabled under existing local government provisions, local government act provisions to make that decision as opposed to it being statutorily required for us.

Um, the mayor has said, and we have consistently said what we do, agree that a CCO model will be, um, the right model for public transport functions, but nonetheless, that doesn't have to be statutorily required. Um, you know, we can make that decision ourselves a, a particular point, and if there are questions we can take those later, um, that we have requested that if it does re is retained as a statutory CCO, then we do request that the bill enables that CCO to be a company under the company's act.

What that does is it allows us as shareholder to, um, have more leverage and levers and controls, for example, like, um, issuing binding directions, um, or having approval rights for major financial transactions. You've also heard, um, there are various places in the legislation, um, where the ministerial approval is required, um, for delegation of functions.

So where council might be delegating functions to that, uh, public transport, CCO, it also requires ministerial approval. Um, again, Auckland Council should be able to determine, uh, want it, what, what it wants to delegate within the direction, um, that has been set by the legislation. Um, also, there will be times we know that there will need to be quick decisions made around delegations, and having additional approvals will create unnecessary time, um, delays and probably resource delays as well.

In terms of decision making, you've heard us talk about the, and the mayor has raised, uh, the, the issue of governing body and local boards, the split. Um, and while we are very clear that we support greater local decision making in transport, um, we do not support the provisions that prescribe that allocation, um, of, of responsibilities to local boards.

Uh, it is, uh, we are concerned that it is too prescriptive. It doesn't, it doesn't allow any flexibility as that starts working out on the ground. Uh, and, and as the mayor said, once you put something in primary legislation, uh, it's almost impossible to change even if there are, um, unintended consequences.

So we believe that it is at the moment, as, as drafted, unworkable in practice, uh, and overcomplicates, uh, the decision making. We gave an example in our submission, um, of roading, for example, around Eden Park. And of course, Eden Park also has a rail, uh, station right next to it too. So you could add that in. You'll have roads that are arterial, so that might be in governing body, and you'll have local roads, um, particularly straight around Eden Park.

And how we can get a, an integrated, um, uh, view and network view, for example, around Eden Park is an example of unnecessarily complicated decision making, um, that would occur as the legislation is currently drafted. So, while, so what we are saying is, rather than having statutory, um, allocation of powers that the council should use, its existing powers under local government act to do that work, uh, and to be able to work that, um, in the most appropriate way.

There's also a bit about bylaws. Again, the mayor raised this at the moment. Uh, it requires, um, that, uh, all local boards agree to a, to a bylaw. That's 21. Um, in our world, uh, again, that's just not workable. We have tried and tested provisions of having local board input, direct input into all bylaw, um, proposals and, and decisions, but the decision is made by the governing body that works.

We think it should just remain, um, uh, under that tried and true, um, method of, of, of developing bylaws. The roading classification framework, again, we do support having a roading classification framework. We have one already. We know NCTA has one that that's standard. The concern we have here is that in the legislation, the framework itself is being used to then allocate these decision making, these decisions to between governing body and local boards.

We don't think that is the right use of it, and therefore we are, um, concerned, um, at that, at that provision. Uh, we have no concerns with having a framework, but not for it to be used for that purpose. Um, there's also a requirement for the minister to approve that framework as well. And again, that seems, um, unnecessary to us.

And just my last point before I, um, uh, to back to Phil to talk a little bit more about some of the employment matters. So just in terms of the transitional provisions, uh, we are proposing or suggesting for the interim CCO board, so the one that will occur on Royal Ascent, um, we propose a five member limit as opposed to the three.

There is substantial work to be done. And when we look at the existing, uh, Auckland Transport Board and the numbers on that, we just think threes are too few. So we would propose, um, up to five, um, on that interim board. We do support the review of the current policies and plans, uh, around transport.

Uh, but again, what we are saying here is that we should, uh, Orland council should be enabled to determine which ones we review at over what timeframe. Uh, and we're also, um, very supportive of the inclusion of a transition plan in the bill. Um, but we are proposing, um, and suggesting that in fact, the chief executive approves that plan as opposed to our governors.

That is the transition plan is a management plan effectively, of how the organization will work to implement the, the, the, the law and the transition. And so we think the chief executive should have sign off, um, of that plan. If you're okay, Mr. Chair, I'll just pass to Phil to, um, put his concluding remarks in.

Thanks. That'd be great. And then we'll go to questions. Thank you. Yeah, so, um, just to comment on the transition and employment provisions. Um, there's clearly a lot riding on the transition being managed well, and, uh, we are supportive of the provision that allows for six months following Royal Ascent.

Um, um, to be blunt, we will try and accelerate, um, the transition a bit. Um, the consequence of that in the round is that people within the Auckland Transport and Auckland Council environment are, are kept in a state of, uh, uncertainty to a degree for upwards of 12 months.

Um, so while the legislation's fine in that, it, it, um, sets out an, um, a, a timeframe, six months following Royals Royal Ascent to have this done. Uh, we want to move a little bit faster, and I can tell the select committee that the planning around this has already well and truly began, uh, begun, um, in a collaborative fashion with, um, Auckland Transport itself.

Um, that includes the functional design for the public transport CCO entity. And the way that arrangements will happen within council. I think you'll understand from an employment or an employer perspective, um, that there's a certain wisdom in moving quickly and not leaving people in limbo for too long.

So, while we support the six month timeframe, I'm letting you know that we're, um, moving a bit faster than that, um, than the, um, the objective, of course is to create sort of two high performing organizations. There'll be a public transport, CCO with, with, we think, something in the region of 300 to 350 staff.

Um, meaning that there are about 14 to 1500 Auckland Transport staff who would come across into the council organization. What we wanted to be clear with you on is that, um, in, and, and we've talked to this in the submission, we, we are well practiced at this type of thing.

There is well established employment law, both, both common law and other, uh, other, um, precedents, um, and, and elsewhere in the legal framework for how this is handled. Um, the, this transport legislation uses slightly different language and some slightly different, um, notions around that, um, equivalency of role, um, uh, comes to mind.

I won't dig into this too much. Our encouragement is that we rely on the existing, uh, framework that already exists for this type of change in organizations, not try and, uh, invent something bespoke, um, uh, for, for this situation. That's, that's kind of a key, a key message.

Um, um, I think I'll, I think I'll leave it there rather than, um, get into a slightly arcane discussion about the Employment Law Pro provisions. But we would welcome opportunity if, if, uh, we need to, to, to clarify, uh, this point because it's, it, it's quite important for how the, how the transition would happen.

Um, one thing I would say is that, um, we, we want to preserve employment wherever possible down through the, uh, transport structures. Um, the mayor will talk to, I'm sure. Um, and, and I agree there's an opportunity here for a bit of a reset and some important change in culture with, um, with, um, with this change.

So, no, no argument there. But we've gotta balance this between, um, the change that we need and the way that transport operates in Auckland, and the need for, uh, preserving, um, some of the key talent that exists at an operational level within, within transport.

So we've got a view of how that might best operate. And, uh, it's reflected in, in this, in the submission. Um, I think one thing I forgot to do, but I'll just wrap up with it, is just I did want to sort of in this environment, think the, uh, ministry of Transport staff who have worked with us, um, up till now, and frankly also the ministers and, and other, um, um, uh, uh, politicians who have been party to all of the work up to this point.

Uh, we haven't sort of, um, got anywhere with that, but I do know that we will need to keep on the conversation. One conversation is not gonna finish, uh, all the, I suspect we will want to, um, discuss with you.

Chair. Thank you, Mr. Mayor, Phil and the rest of the Auckland team. 'cause I think what's really important here is that there is the, the need for efficiency, operational efficiency, but also a level of fairness around that.

So, pleased to hear you, um, talk about that. Can I just pick up on the, um, comments that you've made today, but also in your submission around the role of local boards. So there's good safeguards in this, um, that protect the interests locally, um, to, to decision making. Um, but honestly, um, my experience having worked in this environment for 15 years is that we are best to leave it to us to, to, uh, work that problem locally.

That's basically what you're saying. Uh, yes, we are. So, um, with the primary legislation set, the clear direction or expectations, but the way that that occurs, um, you can leave that to us. Congratulations on your reelection and your, um, thumping majority. Um, and, um, and for your advocacy in this area, I know that you and I have talked for, uh, many months around this, and you've campaigned openly on this, so congratulations for your work getting this bill to this stage.

Do, do you feel confident that this bill as drafted, gives, um, gives that level of accountability and responsiveness back? Okay, very good question. Thank you. Uh, member bid, um, it's a great step forward. Uh, I think the public, as I've said before, are wiser than given, um, than people think that the, the delivery of public transport's going reasonably well in Auckland.

Um, in this 56, I think of them, um, and growing, uh, growing, the chief executive has been unable to answer to me, um, how the decisions are made. really small minded employees actually. Um, so you have have about 10 people to decide anything, so no one's ever responsible. Mm-hmm. So we've looked out the road and they've just done something really stupid out. I'm just fixing on two or three big intersections right outside my office. Mm-hmm. And we think, well, out of these 56 people who do you ask to change that arrow?

Yeah. And it's like Russia, I know where it is, but I dunno who to call. And so we want this to be set up. So what are, because I have experience with other councils beforehand, you set up what are the tasks you're doing, the major tasks you're doing other than delivering public transport, is delivering effective roads that are available for all forms of use. And, and you don't, that this doesn't happen by having someone who's in charge of cycleways, having to compete with someone in charge of cars, this having to compete with someone in charge of buses.
